standard_description: Multiply one-digit whole numbers by multiples of 10 in the range 10–90 (e.g., 9 × 80, 5 × 60) using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
sources: Bay-Williams, J.M., & Kling, G. (2019). Math Fact Fluency: 60+ Games and Assessment Tools to Support Learning and Retention. ASCD.
NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Number and Operations in Base Ten (NBT) K–5, 2015
Van de Walle, J.A., Karp, K.S., & Bay-Williams, J.M. (2013). Elementary and Middle School Mathematics (8th ed.). Pearson.
EngageNY Grade 3 Module 3 Topic G — Multiplication of Single-Digit Factors and Multiples of 10, 2013
Smarter Balanced Assessment Consortium — Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications, 2015

misconceptions.short_label: Forgets to attach 0 in product (3 × 40 = 12 instead of 120)
misconceptions.description: Student computes the basic fact (3 × 4 = 12) but forgets that the second factor was 40, not 4. The 'attach a 0' step is omitted, producing a product 10× too small.
misconceptions.example: 5 × 60 = ? Student computes 5 × 6 = 30 and answers 30 instead of 300.
misconceptions.citation: Bay-Williams & Kling (2019) p.118 — 'The most persistent error in single-digit × multiple-of-10 problems is omission of the trailing zero in the product; students treat the multiple of 10 as its non-zero digit and forget the place-value adjustment.'
misconceptions.remediation: Always say aloud: 'I'm multiplying by SIXTY, not six. So my product needs ONE zero.' Use a place-value chart: write the product in the tens column, then add the 0 in the ones column. Build the habit: 'find the basic fact, then attach the zero.'

misconceptions.description: Student over-applies the 'attach a 0' rule and writes too many zeros. May add a 0 for each factor or may multiply '×10' twice. Result is 10× or 100× too large.
misconceptions.example: 3 × 40 = ? Student computes 3 × 4 = 12 and answers 1200 (added two zeros). Correct: 120.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM NBT Progressions K-5 (2015) p.18 — 'Students who learn the zero-attachment rule mechanically often misapply it by counting both factors\' zeros and the absent zero on the single-digit factor; place-value reasoning prevents this.'
misconceptions.remediation: Count the zeros in the FACTORS only — and only one factor has a zero here (the multiple of 10). One zero in factors = one zero in product. Show with place value: 3 × 40 = 3 × (4 × 10) = (3 × 4) × 10 = 12 × 10 = 120.

misconceptions.description: Student reads 40 as the digit '4' and computes 3 × 4 = 12, ignoring that 40 means 4 tens. Has not yet internalized that multiples of 10 represent groups of ten.
misconceptions.example: 7 × 30 = ? Student reads 30 as 'three' and answers 21 (= 7 × 3). Correct: 210.
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.190 — 'Place-value misreading is more fundamental than zero-attachment: some students don't recognize that 40 means 4 × 10 = 40, treating the digits as separate symbols rather than as a place-value compound.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Read multiples of 10 with their place value: 'forty means 4 tens.' Use base-10 blocks: 7 groups of 4 tens-rods = 28 tens-rods = 280. Rebuild the meaning before applying any rule.

misconceptions.short_label: Place value correct but basic fact wrong
misconceptions.description: Student correctly identifies 'attach a zero' but makes an error in the underlying single-digit multiplication. Especially common with ×6, ×7, ×8, ×9 facts that aren't yet fluent.
misconceptions.example: 8 × 70 = ? Student knows to attach a 0 but says 8 × 7 = 54 (should be 56), so answers 540 instead of 560.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 3 Topic G Teacher Edition — 'Errors in single-digit × multiple-of-10 problems often have two origins: place-value confusion and basic-fact error; isolate which is the cause to remediate effectively.'
misconceptions.remediation: Practice the basic fact first as a separate skill (8 × 7 = 56). Once fluent, the multiple-of-10 extension is automatic. Build a ×6/×7/×8 fluency anchor before attempting these compound problems.

misconceptions.description: In a multi-step problem (e.g., word problem requiring 4 × 30 then add 25), student computes 4 × 30 = 120 correctly, then drops the 0 mid-calculation, answering 12 + 25 = 37 instead of 120 + 25 = 145.
misconceptions.example: Word problem: 'Buy 4 boxes of 30 markers and 25 extra. Total?' Student does 4×30=120 ✓ but writes 12 below and computes 12+25=37. Correct: 145.
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.193 — 'Working memory limitations in multi-step computations often cause students to drop place-value markers (zeros) in intermediate results; explicit re-reading of the partial product before continuing prevents this.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Always rewrite the intermediate result clearly with all zeros before continuing. Underline the partial product and check: 'Did I keep all the zeros?' Use a place-value column layout to keep digits aligned.

misconceptions.description: Student computes 4 × 50 = 200 correctly but stumbles on 40 × 5 (same product mathematically). The position of the multiple of 10 (left vs right) creates confusion despite commutativity.
misconceptions.example: 4 × 50 = 200 ✓. But 40 × 5 = ? Student answers 20 (treats 40 as 4) or 2000 (extra zeros). Correct: 200.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM NBT Progressions K-5 (2015) p.19 — 'Students who master one form (single-digit × multiple-of-10) often struggle with the commuted form (multiple-of-10 × single-digit); explicit teaching of commutativity in this context is needed.'
misconceptions.remediation: Always rewrite to put the multiple of 10 second: 40 × 5 → 5 × 40. Then apply the standard rule: 5 × 4 = 20, attach 0 → 200. Practice both directions on the same product.

misconceptions.description: Student tries to use distributive property mechanically by 'splitting' 40 into 4 + 0 instead of 4 × 10. Computes 3 × 4 + 3 × 0 = 12 + 0 = 12, missing that 40 = 4 × 10 (not 4 + 0).
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 3 Topic G Teacher Edition — 'Distributive misapplication occurs when students split a multiple of 10 additively rather than multiplicatively; emphasize that 40 = 4 × 10, not 4 + 0.'
misconceptions.remediation: Always rewrite multiples of 10 in factored form: 40 = 4 × 10, 60 = 6 × 10. Then use the associative property: a × (b × 10) = (a × b) × 10. Never split as a + 0.
